siliconalley:client_greeting: Good to hear from you. Keep shipping quality and the contracts will keep coming.

help_siliconalley-overview_content:
**Silicon Alley** adds three IT companies to the game. Found one in an **Office** building and staff it with **Programmers** hired from **City Workforce Inc.**, working at **Computer Workstations**.

Each studio designs software projects that ship for a quality-scored payout, then earn recurring support income from their installed base — scaled by reputation and neighbourhood competition.

**Business types**

- [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio)
- [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity)
- [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio)

**Goods & services**

- [Software License](siliconalley-softwarelicense)
- [Security Audit](siliconalley-securityaudit)
- [Video Game](siliconalley-videogame)

**Guides**

- [Design Wizard](siliconalley-wizard) — plan and design a project

More guides — the economy & market, and getting started — are on the way.
help_siliconalley-softwarestudio_content:
**Software Engineering Studio** businesses operate out of office buildings.

Your team designs and ships business software as a digital product, so customers are reached through releases rather than walk-in foot traffic.

The business requires the following furniture to function:

* [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

Businesses of this type can sell:

* [Software License](siliconalley-softwarelicense)

Employees with the following skills can be assigned:

* [Programmer](skill-programmer)
* [Graphic Designer](skill-graphicdesigner)

**How the studio works**

The studio works on one project at a time. Open the design window with **F9** to plan and steer it, and staff it with Programmers hired from City Workforce Inc. seated at a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation). The project moves through four stages that you push forward: **Design** (Graphic Designers lead the concept), **Development** (*Start development* confirms the design), **Testing** (*Send to testing* to burn down bugs), and **Release** — ship whenever you like, but the later you ship the higher the quality. A release ships a [Software License](siliconalley-softwarelicense), pays a one-off launch sum, grows your installed base, and returns the studio to idle for the next project.

**Designing a project**

The design wizard turns choices into quality and reach: pick **features** (Cloud Sync, Plugin API, Enterprise SSO…) to raise the quality ceiling, **build or license tools**, target **platforms** for wider reach, choose an **audience segment** (a price-versus-volume trade-off), and resolve **dependencies**. Putting more effort into the features the market wants most lifts both quality and sales.

**Earning**

Launch pay scales with **quality**, your **reputation**, the **market-demand** cycle and neighbourhood **competition**, and is dented by residual **bugs** (which also lower the 0–10 **review** score). After launch the installed base pays recurring **support income**; ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep it fresh, sign **publisher deals** for a deadline bonus, or take fixed-scope **contracts** from the phone. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview) for the bigger picture.
help_siliconalley-cybersecurity_content:
**Cyber Security Firm** businesses operate out of office buildings.

Your team delivers security work — sold as audits — as a digital service, so clients are handled through engagements rather than walk-in foot traffic.

The business requires the following furniture to function:

* [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

Businesses of this type can sell:

* [Security Audit](siliconalley-securityaudit)

Employees with the following skills can be assigned:

* [Programmer](skill-programmer)

**How the studio works**

The firm works on one engagement at a time. Open the design window with **F9** and staff it with Programmers hired from City Workforce Inc. seated at a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation). Unlike the other studios this one has no separate design discipline — your programmers plan the concept themselves. The project moves through four stages you push forward: **Design**, **Development** (*Start development* confirms the design), **Testing** (*Send to testing* to burn down bugs), and **Release** — ship whenever you like, with later ships scoring higher quality. A release ships a [Security Audit](siliconalley-securityaudit), pays a one-off launch sum, grows your installed base, and returns the firm to idle.

**Designing a project**

The design wizard turns choices into quality and reach: pick **features** (Threat Intel Feed, Compliance Reporting, Zero-Trust Module…) to raise the quality ceiling, **build or license tools**, target **platforms**, choose an **audience segment** (price versus volume), and resolve **dependencies**. Allocating effort to the features the market wants most lifts both quality and sales.

**Earning**

Launch pay scales with **quality**, **reputation**, the **market-demand** cycle and neighbourhood **competition**, and is dented by residual **bugs** (which also lower the 0–10 **review** score). The installed base then pays recurring **support income**; ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep it fresh, sign **publisher deals**, or take fixed-scope **contracts** from the phone. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-gamestudio_content:
**Game Developer Studio** businesses operate out of office buildings.

Your team builds and ships video games as a digital product, reached through releases rather than walk-in foot traffic.

The business requires the following furniture to function:

* [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

Businesses of this type can sell:

* [Video Game](siliconalley-videogame)

Employees with the following skills can be assigned:

* [Programmer](skill-programmer)
* [Graphic Designer](skill-graphicdesigner)

**How the studio works**

The studio works on one game at a time. Open the design window with **F9** and staff it with Programmers hired from City Workforce Inc. seated at a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation). The project moves through four stages you push forward: **Design** (Graphic Designers lead the concept), **Development** (*Start development* confirms the design), **Testing** (*Send to testing* to burn down bugs), and **Release** — ship whenever you like, with later ships scoring higher quality. A release ships a [Video Game](siliconalley-videogame), pays a one-off launch sum, grows your player base, and returns the studio to idle.

**Designing a project**

The design wizard turns choices into quality and reach: pick **features** (Advanced Graphics, Multiplayer Netcode, Mod Support…) to raise the quality ceiling, **build or license tools**, target **platforms** (PC, Console, Mobile, Web) for wider reach, choose an **audience segment** (price versus volume), and resolve **dependencies**. Putting effort into the features players want most lifts both quality and sales.

**Earning**

Launch pay scales with **quality**, **reputation**, the **market-demand** cycle and neighbourhood **competition**, and is dented by residual **bugs** (which also lower the 0–10 **review** score). Your player base then pays recurring **support income**; ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep it fresh, sign **publisher deals**, or take fixed-scope **contracts** from the phone. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-softwarelicense_conten:
**Software License** is the product shipped by the [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio) — a Silicon Alley business you run from an office.

Unlike a physical good it isn't stocked on a shelf or built from a Factory recipe. Your team designs and develops it as a project; when you **release** it, it sells for a one-off, quality-scored launch sum and grows your installed base.

After launch the product keeps earning recurring **support income** from everyone who owns it, and you can ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep that revenue alive. Launch pay and support both scale with quality, reputation, market demand and competition.

See the [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio) page for the full project loop, or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-securityaudit_content:
**Security Audit** is the service shipped by the [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity) — a Silicon Alley business you run from an office.

Unlike a physical good it isn't stocked on a shelf or built from a Factory recipe. Your team scopes and delivers it as a project; when you **release** it, it pays a one-off, quality-scored launch sum and grows your installed base of clients.

After launch it keeps earning recurring **support income**, and you can ship **updates** and follow-up **engagements** to keep that revenue alive. Launch pay and support both scale with quality, reputation, market demand and competition.

See the [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity) page for the full project loop, or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-videogame_content:
**Video Game** is the product shipped by the [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) — a Silicon Alley business you run from an office.

Unlike a physical good it isn't stocked on a shelf or built from a Factory recipe. Your team designs and develops it as a project; when you **release** it, it sells for a one-off, quality-scored launch sum and grows your player base.

After launch it keeps earning recurring **support income** from its players, and you can ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep that revenue alive. Launch pay and support both scale with quality, reputation, market demand and competition.

See the [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) page for the full project loop, or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-wizard_content:
**Design Wizard**

Every Silicon Alley project starts in the design wizard — the step-by-step planner inside your studio's design window. Open it with **F9** (or from a project notification), then click **Start project** to enter the **Design** stage and the wizard appears. Move with **Back** and **Next**; on the final step **Start development** locks your plan and your programmers begin building it.

The wizard has five steps — **Concept**, **Dependencies**, **Components**, **Market** and **Summary**. Your choices feed two things: the project's **size** (how long it takes to build) and its **quality ceiling** (the score its quality can climb toward during Development and Testing).

**Concept**

* **Scope** — *Quick win*, *Standard* or *Ambitious*. Quick is half the work for lower pay but barely minds rivals; Standard is the baseline; Ambitious is twice the work for premium pay, but a crowded neighbourhood bites harder. Scope locks when development begins, so changing it only affects your next project.
* **Product name** — name the release (leave blank for a default); it's recorded in the studio's release history.
* **Design focus** — a Polish ↔ Speed slider that biases the Design phase.

**Dependencies — features & tools**

* **Features** — pick from five per studio (e.g. Cloud Sync, Multiplayer Netcode, Zero-Trust Module). Each raises the **quality ceiling** but also adds **size** (more dev time). None are mandatory — more features mean a better but bigger product.
* **Editors & tools** — each tool cycles **Off → License → Build & Own**. *Build* costs one-off R&D cash, then it's a free, reusable studio asset forever. *License* costs nothing up front but takes an ongoing **royalty** from launch and support revenue. Either way using a tool raises the ceiling — only *licensed* tools charge royalty.
* **Coverage** — each feature you picked needs a covering tool. An **uncovered** feature lowers the quality ceiling (−12% each). Full coverage — or a project with no features — carries no penalty, so pick features you can back with tools.

**Components**

Build-or-buy the product's underlying stack (OS runtime, framework, backend…). Each slot cycles **Off → license a vendor → build in-house**. *Building* costs one-off R&D cash, adds a quality bonus, and becomes a reusable asset; *licensing a vendor* adds a quality bonus but takes an ongoing royalty.

**Market**

* **Operating systems** — toggle the platforms you ship on. More platforms **widen launch reach** (a bigger installed base) but add **porting** work (more size). With none selected you ship to a single home platform — reach is never zero. Reach grows your installed base, not the cash price.
* **Segment** — who it's for, as a price ↔ volume trade-off: *Broad* (neutral), *Enterprise* (high price, low volume), *Prosumer* (mid), *Consumer* (low price, high volume). Price scales the launch payout; volume scales the installed base.
* **Allocation & demand** — set how much to weight each selected feature, then check the demand panel: the market wants a **rotating mix** of product aspects that drifts over time. Matching what it wants — versus an even split — lifts **both** quality and launch revenue; a neutral split has no effect.

**Summary**

A read-only review before you commit: scope, size and ship estimate, the projected **quality ceiling**, **dependency coverage**, **up-front cost** (R&D for anything you build), **ongoing royalties** (from anything you license), and the **reachable market** (platform reach × segment volume, with price shown separately). Happy with it? **Start development** locks the design and coding begins.

**The quality ceiling**

Most choices feed one number — the quality ceiling your project climbs toward in Development and Testing. **Features, tools, built or licensed components, and a well-targeted feature allocation raise it; features left without a covering tool lower it.** Aim for features you can cover, a stack that fits your budget, and a market you've actually targeted.

See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview), or a studio page — [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio), [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity), [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) — for what each studio designs.
